title: Signs You Need Glasses: 10 Symptoms That Mean It's Time for an Eye Exam
description: Squinting, headaches, blurry vision? Here are 10 clear signs you might need glasses — and why booking an eye exam is the smart next step.
tldr: Common signs you need glasses include squinting, frequent headaches, blurry vision at distance or near, eye fatigue after screen use, difficulty driving at night, and holding books at arm's length. If you're experiencing any of these regularly, an eye exam can determine whether glasses would help.

tldrStats.label: Common signs covered in this guide
Of adults need some form of vision correction
Recommended eye exam frequency for adults

definition.text: Signs you need glasses are observable symptoms of uncorrected refractive errors (myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, or presbyopia) that indicate a person would benefit from corrective lenses. These symptoms include blurred vision, squinting, headaches, eye strain, difficulty with night vision, and trouble seeing at specific distances. A comprehensive eye exam by an optometrist is required for diagnosis and prescription.

sections.h2: 1. You Squint to See Things Clearly
sections.content:
Squinting is your eyes' built-in cheat code. By narrowing the opening that light passes through, squinting temporarily sharpens focus — similar to how a camera's aperture works. If you catch yourself squinting to read signs, see the TV, or make out faces across a room, your eyes are compensating for a focusing error that glasses can fix.

Occasional squinting in bright sunlight is normal. Frequent squinting in normal lighting — especially to see distant objects — is a reliable signal that something's off with your vision.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Headaches centred around the forehead, temples, or behind the eyes are one of the most common signs of uncorrected vision problems. Your eye muscles strain to compensate for imperfect focus, and that sustained effort triggers tension headaches.

These headaches typically worsen through the day (as eye strain accumulates) and feel better after rest or sleep. If you get headaches regularly and they correlate with reading, screen time, or other visual tasks, vision correction might solve the problem entirely.

Of course, headaches have many possible causes. But if yours follow a pattern tied to visual tasks, an eye exam is a worthwhile first step.

If faraway objects look fuzzy — street signs, faces across a room, the scoreboard at a hockey game — you're likely nearsighted (myopic). Myopia is the most common refractive error, affecting roughly 30% of Canadians.

Nearsightedness typically develops in childhood or adolescence but can worsen into early adulthood. Some people don't realize how blurry their distance vision is until they try on glasses for the first time and suddenly see leaves on trees as individual leaves rather than a green blob.

If you find yourself holding books, menus, or your phone further and further away to get the text in focus, you're likely developing presbyopia — the age-related stiffening of the eye's lens that affects near focus.

Presbyopia is not a disease; it's a completely normal part of aging that starts around 40 and progresses until about 65. Almost everyone gets it. Reading glasses, bifocals, or progressive lenses correct it completely.

If you already wear distance glasses and now struggle with near vision too, progressive lenses combine both corrections in one pair. Your optometrist can measure your near addition power during a comprehensive eye exam.

sections.h2: 5. Eye Fatigue and Strain After Screen Time
sections.content:
Tired, achy, or burning eyes after a few hours of computer work often point to uncorrected or under-corrected vision. Your eyes are working harder than they should to maintain focus, and that extra effort shows up as fatigue.

This is especially common in people with mild prescriptions who "get by" without glasses. Their vision is technically functional, but the constant compensating effort drains their eyes by mid-afternoon.

A proper prescription — even a mild one — can make a dramatic difference in daily comfort. If screen time leaves you feeling wiped out, consider whether your eyes might be the bottleneck.

Night driving problems — halos around headlights, difficulty seeing road markings, reduced contrast in dim conditions — are a common early sign that your vision needs correction.

Your pupils dilate in low light, which amplifies any refractive error. A mild prescription that doesn't bother you during the day can become genuinely problematic at night when your pupils are wide open. Astigmatism is particularly noticeable at night, causing starbursts and streaky halos around light sources.

If night driving has gotten uncomfortable, mention it to your optometrist. Even a small correction can dramatically improve nighttime visual clarity.

sections.h2: 7. You Sit Too Close to Screens
sections.content:
If you've caught yourself leaning closer and closer to your computer monitor or sitting unusually close to the TV, your eyes may be struggling with distance focus. This is particularly common in children — teachers and parents often notice it before the child does.

In kids, this can signal myopia that's developing or worsening. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends annual eye exams for children, and Alberta covers eye exams for children under 18 through Alberta Health.

sections.h2: 8. You Lose Your Place While Reading
sections.content:
Consistently losing your place while reading, skipping lines, or needing to use your finger to track text can indicate an uncorrected refractive error or an eye coordination issue. Your eyes may be struggling to maintain stable focus or work together smoothly.

This is different from occasional distraction — it's a pattern of physical difficulty keeping text in focus and tracking it across a page. An eye exam can determine whether the problem is optical (fixable with glasses) or related to eye coordination (which may need vision therapy).

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. sections.h2: 9. One Eye Is Doing All the Work
sections.content:
If you instinctively close or cover one eye to see more clearly — when reading, looking at a screen, or watching something in the distance — one eye may have significantly different vision than the other.

Having different prescriptions in each eye (anisometropia) is very common. Your brain naturally favours the eye with better focus, and you may not realize the other eye is underperforming until it's tested. Glasses correct each eye independently, balancing the workload so both eyes contribute to a clear, comfortable image.

sections.h2: 10. It's Been More Than Two Years Since Your Last Eye Exam
sections.content:
This isn't technically a "symptom," but it's worth including: if you haven't had an eye exam in over two years, you may have a vision problem you're not aware of. Many refractive changes happen gradually enough that your brain compensates without you noticing. You adapt to slightly blurry vision and assume it's normal.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every one to two years for adults aged 20-64, and annually for adults over 65, children, and people with diabetes or known eye conditions.

Regular exams also screen for eye health conditions like glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration — conditions that can develop without symptoms until significant damage has occurred.

If it's been a while, book an eye exam at Charm Optical. The team will check both your prescription and your overall eye health, so you know exactly where things stand.

sections.h2: What to Do If You Recognize These Signs
sections.content:
The answer is straightforward: get an eye exam. Self-diagnosis isn't reliable for vision problems because your brain is remarkably good at compensating for gradual changes. A comprehensive eye exam includes objective measurements that show exactly what's happening with your vision.

In Alberta, eye exams for children under 18 are covered by Alberta Health. Adults with employer insurance typically have coverage for regular exams. Even without insurance, an eye exam is one of the most cost-effective health investments you can make.

If glasses are recommended, your optician will help you choose frames and lenses that match your prescription, lifestyle, and budget. For many people, getting their first pair of glasses is a revelatory experience — you don't know what you've been missing until you can see clearly.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

faqs.q: At what age do most people need glasses?
faqs.a: It varies widely. Many people need glasses starting in childhood or adolescence (typically for myopia). Those with good distance vision often need reading glasses starting around age 40 due to presbyopia. By age 65, the vast majority of people use some form of vision correction.

faqs.q: Can you need glasses even if you can see 'fine'?
faqs.a: Yes. Mild prescriptions, mild astigmatism, and early presbyopia can cause symptoms like headaches and eye fatigue without obviously blurry vision. Your brain compensates for minor issues, so you might feel 'fine' while your eyes are working harder than they need to.

faqs.q: How often should I get my eyes checked in Alberta?
faqs.a: The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends every 1-2 years for adults aged 20-64, and annually for children, seniors over 65, and people with diabetes. Eye exams for children under 18 are covered by Alberta Health. Book at Charm Optical in South Edmonton for a comprehensive exam.

faqs.q: Can eye strain from screens mean I need glasses?
faqs.a: Possibly. Screen-related eye strain is often caused by uncorrected or under-corrected vision. Even a mild prescription can significantly reduce eye fatigue. An eye exam will determine whether glasses would help or whether the issue is purely related to screen habits.

faqs.q: Do I need a referral to get an eye exam in Alberta?
faqs.a: No referral is needed to see an optometrist in Alberta. You can book directly with any optometric practice. At Charm Optical, you can book online at see.charmoptical.ca or call (780) 490-0090.
